title: Networking
description: From packets to sockets: master how data moves across networks — IP, TCP/UDP, DNS, HTTP(S), TLS — and the tools to see it all.

sections.title: Networking Foundations
sections.description: Build a mental model of how data travels across the Internet before diving into any single protocol.

topics.title: How the Internet Works
topics.description: A big-picture tour of the Internet: clients, servers, ISPs, backbones, and how a request travels from your machine to a server and back.
topics.why: Every debugging session and design decision in networking sits on top of this mental model. Once you can narrate what happens when you load a web page, every later topic slots into a place you already understand.
learn: Sketch the journey of a request from your browser to a web server and back, naming each hop
Distinguish clients, servers, routers, and switches, and what each one does
Explain what an ISP is and how networks of networks interconnect
Describe at a high level what happens when you type a URL and press Enter

resources.label: Cloudflare Learning — How does the Internet work?
Khan Academy — Computers and the Internet

criteria: You can narrate 'what happens when I visit a website' end to end in your own words
You can explain the difference between a router and a switch
You can describe how independent networks form the Internet

topics.title: The OSI Model
topics.description: The seven-layer reference model that splits networking into physical, data link, network, transport, session, presentation, and application concerns.
topics.why: The OSI model is the shared vocabulary of networking: 'layer 4 load balancer', 'L7 firewall', 'layer 2 switch'. Knowing which layer a problem lives at is the fastest way to narrow down any network issue.
learn: Memorize the seven layers in order and one responsibility of each
Place familiar things on the model: Ethernet, IP, TCP, TLS, HTTP
Practice layer-by-layer troubleshooting: is the cable up, is IP reachable, is the port open, does the app respond?
Learn why the model is a teaching abstraction, not an exact description of real stacks

resources.label: Cloudflare Learning — What is the OSI model?
Wizard Zines — Julia Evans networking zines

criteria: You can name all seven layers and give an example protocol or device at each
You can assign a real-world bug (e.g. 'connection refused') to a likely layer
You can explain what 'L4 vs L7 load balancing' means

topics.title: The TCP/IP Model
topics.description: The four-layer model (link, internet, transport, application) that actually describes how the Internet protocol suite is built.
topics.why: Real systems are engineered against TCP/IP, not OSI. Understanding it tells you exactly which protocol owns which job — and why IP does not guarantee delivery but TCP does.
learn: Map the four TCP/IP layers to the seven OSI layers
Identify the core protocol at each layer: Ethernet/Wi-Fi, IP, TCP/UDP, HTTP/DNS
Explain the end-to-end principle: smart edges, dumb (fast) core
Describe which guarantees each layer does and does not provide

resources.label: IETF — RFC 1122: Requirements for Internet Hosts

criteria: You can draw the TCP/IP stack and place IP, TCP, UDP, DNS, and HTTP on it
You can explain why IP alone can lose, duplicate, or reorder packets
You can articulate the end-to-end principle with an example

topics.title: Packets & Encapsulation
topics.description: How data is wrapped layer by layer: application payloads inside TCP/UDP segments, inside IP packets, inside Ethernet frames.
topics.why: Encapsulation is the trick that makes layering work, and it is exactly what you see when you open a packet capture. Understanding headers, MAC vs IP vs port addressing, and MTU explains a whole class of mysterious production issues.
learn: Trace one HTTP request as it gets wrapped in TCP, IP, and Ethernet headers
Distinguish the three addresses in play: MAC address, IP address, port number
Learn what MTU and fragmentation are and why huge packets cause problems
Explain what each device (switch, router, host) reads and rewrites in transit

resources.label: Cloudflare Learning — What is a packet?

criteria: You can draw the header stack of a single HTTP-over-TCP packet
You can explain when MAC addresses change in transit but IP addresses do not
You can define MTU and describe one symptom of exceeding it

sections.title: IP Addressing & Routing
sections.description: Learn how machines are addressed, how networks are carved up, and how packets find their way.

topics.title: IPv4 Addressing
topics.description: The 32-bit addressing scheme of IPv4: dotted-decimal notation, network vs host portions, and special addresses like loopback and broadcast.
topics.why: Every server config, firewall rule, and cloud VPC you will ever touch speaks IPv4. Being fluent in reading addresses and masks is table stakes for backend, DevOps, and security work.
learn: Convert IPv4 addresses between dotted-decimal and binary
Split an address into network and host parts using a subnet mask
Memorize the special ranges: 127.0.0.1 loopback, broadcast, 0.0.0.0, link-local
Inspect your own machine's addresses with `ip addr` (or `ifconfig`)

resources.label: Cloudflare Learning — What is the Internet Protocol?

criteria: You can convert any IPv4 address to binary and back without a tool
You can identify the network and host portion given an address and mask
You can explain what 127.0.0.1 and 0.0.0.0 mean in a server config

topics.title: Subnetting & CIDR
topics.description: Dividing networks with CIDR notation (like 10.0.0.0/16): calculating subnet sizes, ranges, and usable host counts.
topics.why: CIDR blocks are everywhere: cloud VPCs, Kubernetes pod ranges, firewall allowlists, VPN configs. Engineers who can subnet in their head design cleaner networks and spot overlapping-range bugs instantly.
learn: Read /8 through /32 prefixes and compute how many hosts each allows
Calculate the network address, broadcast address, and usable range for a given CIDR block
Split a /16 into smaller subnets for a multi-tier network design
Detect overlapping CIDR ranges and explain why they break routing
criteria: You can compute the host range of any /20–/30 network on paper
You designed a subnet plan splitting one block into at least four subnets
You can explain why 10.0.0.0/24 and 10.0.0.128/25 overlap

topics.title: IPv6
topics.description: The 128-bit successor to IPv4: hex notation, address abbreviation rules, address types, and why NAT is largely unnecessary in v6.
topics.why: IPv6 already carries a huge share of Internet traffic, and mobile networks and clouds increasingly default to it. Services that mishandle v6 (or dual-stack) hit real user-facing bugs.
learn: Read and abbreviate IPv6 addresses (zero compression, ::)
Learn the main address types: global unicast, link-local (fe80::), loopback (::1)
Understand dual-stack operation and how clients pick between v4 and v6
Check your own machine and a public site for IPv6 connectivity
criteria: You can expand and abbreviate any IPv6 address correctly
You can explain the difference between link-local and global unicast addresses
You can describe how a dual-stack client chooses a protocol

topics.title: NAT & Private Networks
topics.description: Private address ranges (RFC 1918) and Network Address Translation: how many devices share one public IP, and what that breaks.
topics.why: NAT is why your laptop's 192.168.x.x address works on the Internet — and why peer-to-peer apps, self-hosted servers, and webhooks need port forwarding or tunnels. It shapes how you deploy anything reachable from outside.
learn: Memorize the RFC 1918 private ranges: 10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16
Trace how source NAT rewrites addresses and ports on outbound traffic
Explain why inbound connections fail behind NAT and how port forwarding fixes it
Find your private and public IP and explain why they differ
criteria: You can list the three private IPv4 ranges from memory
You can diagram how NAT translates an outbound connection
You can explain why a webhook can't reach a service behind NAT and name two workarounds

topics.title: Routing Basics
topics.description: How packets choose their path: routing tables, default gateways, longest-prefix match, and a glimpse of BGP between networks.
topics.why: When 'the network is down', it is usually a routing problem — a wrong gateway, a missing route, a misconfigured VPC route table. Reading a routing table is a superpower in cloud and on-prem work alike.
learn: Print and interpret your machine's routing table with `ip route`
Explain the default gateway and longest-prefix-match selection
Follow a packet across multiple routers with traceroute
Read a one-paragraph overview of BGP and why route leaks cause outages
criteria: You can read your routing table and identify the default gateway
You can predict which route wins for a given destination using longest-prefix match
You ran traceroute to a public host and explained each hop

sections.title: Transport Layer: TCP & UDP
sections.description: Understand the two workhorses of the transport layer and the trade-offs between reliability and speed.

topics.title: Ports & Sockets (the Concept)
topics.description: How ports multiplex many conversations over one IP address, and how a socket — the 5-tuple of protocol, addresses, and ports — identifies a connection.
topics.why: 'Address already in use', 'connection refused', 'port 8080 vs 80' — daily developer life is full of port and socket concepts. This is also the exact vocabulary the sockets API uses later.
learn: Explain what a port is and memorize common ones: 22, 53, 80, 443
Define a connection as the 5-tuple (protocol, src IP, src port, dst IP, dst port)
Distinguish listening sockets from established connections
List the sockets open on your machine with `ss -tulpn`
criteria: You can name the standard port for SSH, DNS, HTTP, and HTTPS
You can explain why two browser tabs to the same site are distinct connections
You inspected listening ports on your own machine and identified each process

topics.title: TCP Deep Dive
topics.description: The reliable, ordered, byte-stream protocol: three-way handshake, sequence and acknowledgment numbers, retransmission, and connection teardown.
topics.why: TCP underlies HTTP, SSH, databases — nearly everything. Understanding handshakes, ACKs, and states like TIME_WAIT lets you decode packet captures and diagnose timeouts, resets, and slow connections with confidence.
learn: Walk through the SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK handshake and what state each side holds
Explain sequence/acknowledgment numbers and how lost segments are retransmitted
Trace connection teardown (FIN/ACK) and what TIME_WAIT is for
Interpret RST packets and the classic errors: connection refused vs timeout
Observe a real handshake in a packet capture or with `ss` states
criteria: You can draw the three-way handshake and teardown from memory
You can explain the difference between 'connection refused' and 'connection timed out' at the packet level
You can describe what TIME_WAIT is and why it exists

topics.title: UDP Essentials
topics.description: The minimal, connectionless transport: no handshake, no ordering, no retransmission — just datagrams with ports and a checksum.
topics.why: DNS, video calls, game servers, and QUIC/HTTP-3 all ride on UDP because sometimes losing a packet beats waiting for it. Knowing when UDP's trade-offs win makes you a better systems designer.
learn: Compare the UDP header to TCP's and note everything UDP leaves out
List what applications must handle themselves over UDP: loss, ordering, duplication
Identify real protocols built on UDP: DNS, DHCP, QUIC, WebRTC media
Explain why UDP is both great for latency and a favorite for DDoS amplification
criteria: You can list three guarantees TCP provides that UDP does not
You can name two applications where UDP is the right choice and justify why
You can explain how a UDP-based protocol might implement its own reliability

topics.title: Flow & Congestion Control
topics.description: How TCP avoids overwhelming receivers (flow control via windows) and the network itself (congestion control: slow start, congestion avoidance).
topics.why: This is why file transfers ramp up gradually, why latency spikes under load, and why long-distance connections feel slow. It is the key to reasoning about throughput, bufferbloat, and 'why is this download slow?'.
learn: Distinguish flow control (receiver window) from congestion control (network limit)
Walk through slow start and congestion avoidance as the window grows and shrinks
Understand how packet loss and RTT determine maximum throughput
Skim what modern algorithms like CUBIC and BBR improve
criteria: You can explain flow control vs congestion control in one sentence each
You can sketch how the congestion window changes across slow start and a loss event
You can explain why high-latency links reduce TCP throughput

sections.title: Naming the Network: DNS
sections.description: Master the distributed database that turns names into addresses — and breaks everything when it misbehaves.

topics.title: DNS Fundamentals
topics.description: How name resolution works: the hierarchy from root servers to TLDs to authoritative servers, and the role of recursive resolvers.
topics.why: 'It's always DNS' is a meme because it's true — DNS sits in front of every request your systems make. Understanding the resolution chain turns spooky outages into ten-minute diagnoses.
learn: Trace a full resolution: stub resolver → recursive resolver → root → TLD → authoritative
Distinguish recursive resolvers from authoritative name servers
Explain why DNS typically uses UDP port 53 and when it falls back to TCP
Resolve a name step by step using `dig +trace`
criteria: You can diagram the full resolution path for a fresh domain lookup
You can explain the difference between a recursive and an authoritative server
You ran `dig +trace` and explained each delegation step

topics.title: DNS Record Types
topics.description: The records that make up a zone: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, NS, SOA — what each maps and where each is used.
topics.why: Deploying a website, verifying a domain, setting up email, pointing at a load balancer — all of it is editing DNS records. Knowing the record types (and CNAME's sharp edges) prevents costly misconfigurations.
learn: Define A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, NS, and SOA records and their purposes
Query each record type for a real domain with dig
Learn the CNAME-at-apex restriction and why providers offer ALIAS/ANAME workarounds
Read a zone file and explain every line
criteria: You can pick the correct record type for five common scenarios without looking it up
You queried A, MX, TXT, and NS records for a domain and interpreted the answers
You can explain why a CNAME cannot coexist with other records at the same name

topics.title: DNS Caching, TTLs & Security
topics.description: How TTLs control caching at every layer, and how DNSSEC, DNS-over-HTTPS, and DNS-over-TLS harden a protocol designed without security.
topics.why: TTLs decide how fast your DNS changes propagate (and how bad a mistake lingers), while cache poisoning and plaintext lookups are real attack surfaces. Modern infra work expects you to reason about both.
learn: Explain how TTLs drive caching in resolvers, operating systems, and browsers
Plan a low-downtime DNS migration by lowering TTLs ahead of a cutover
Understand cache poisoning and how DNSSEC's signatures counter it
Compare DNS-over-HTTPS and DNS-over-TLS and what they protect against
criteria: You can explain why a DNS change 'hasn't propagated' and estimate when it will
You planned (or executed) a TTL-lowering migration strategy
You can describe what DoH encrypts that classic DNS does not

sections.title: The Web: HTTP & TLS
sections.description: Go deep on the protocols that carry the web — and the cryptography that keeps it private.

topics.title: HTTP Fundamentals
topics.description: The request/response protocol of the web: methods, status codes, URLs, and the anatomy of HTTP messages.
topics.why: Every API you build or consume is HTTP. Fluency in methods, status codes, and message structure is the difference between guessing at API bugs and reading them straight off the wire.
learn: Dissect a raw HTTP request and response: start line, headers, body
Learn the core methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) and their semantics, including idempotency
Memorize the status code classes (1xx–5xx) and the famous individuals: 200, 201, 301, 304, 400, 401, 403, 404, 429, 500, 502, 503
Send a hand-written request with netcat or curl and read the raw response
criteria: You can write a valid HTTP/1.1 request by hand from memory
You can explain idempotency and classify each method
You can name the right status code for eight common API situations

topics.title: Headers, Cookies & Caching
topics.description: The header fields that control content negotiation, state (cookies and their security attributes), and HTTP caching semantics.
topics.why: Headers are where real-world HTTP problems live: broken caching, CORS errors, insecure cookies, wrong content types. Mastering them unlocks faster sites and fewer security findings.
learn: Inspect request/response headers in browser devtools and with curl -v
Learn cookie attributes — Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite, Domain, Path — and what each defends against
Understand Cache-Control, ETag, and conditional requests (304 Not Modified)
Get a working mental model of CORS: origins, preflights, and the headers involved
criteria: You can configure a cookie correctly for a session and justify each attribute
You can explain how an ETag round-trip produces a 304
You diagnosed (or simulated) a CORS error and fixed it at the server

topics.title: TLS Fundamentals
topics.description: The protocol that encrypts the Internet: the TLS handshake, symmetric vs asymmetric cryptography, and what confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity mean.
topics.why: TLS fronts every serious service, and its failures — expired certs, protocol mismatches, handshake errors — land on developers to debug. Understanding the handshake demystifies the whole 'padlock' stack.
learn: Explain the goals: confidentiality, integrity, and authentication
Walk through a TLS 1.3 handshake: key exchange, certificate verification, session keys
Understand why asymmetric crypto bootstraps a symmetric session
Inspect a live handshake with `openssl s_client -connect host:443`

resources.label: Cloudflare Learning — What happens in a TLS handshake?

criteria: You can narrate a TLS 1.3 handshake step by step
You can explain why TLS uses both asymmetric and symmetric cryptography
You connected to a server with openssl s_client and read the negotiated protocol and cipher

topics.title: Certificates & PKI
topics.description: X.509 certificates, certificate authorities, chains of trust, and automated issuance with ACME/Let's Encrypt.
topics.why: Certificate expiry is one of the most common causes of self-inflicted outages. Knowing how chains, SANs, and issuance work lets you set up TLS correctly once and automate it forever.
learn: Read a certificate: subject, SANs, issuer, validity period, chain to a root CA
Explain how a browser validates a chain of trust
Issue a free certificate with Let's Encrypt / certbot and set up auto-renewal
Understand what goes wrong: expired certs, name mismatches, incomplete chains, self-signed warnings

resources.label: Cloudflare Learning — What is an SSL certificate?

criteria: You obtained and installed a real certificate with automated renewal
You can explain the chain of trust from leaf to root CA
You can diagnose the three most common certificate errors from their messages

topics.title: HTTPS, HTTP/2 & HTTP/3
topics.description: How HTTP rides on TLS, and how HTTP/2 (multiplexing over TCP) and HTTP/3 (QUIC over UDP) reinvented web transport for speed.
topics.why: Version choice has real performance consequences: head-of-line blocking, connection setup cost, mobile roaming. Modern web performance work assumes you know what each version fixes.
learn: Explain what HTTPS adds over HTTP and why every site should use it
Understand HTTP/2 multiplexing and the TCP head-of-line blocking it cannot fix
Learn how HTTP/3/QUIC moves to UDP to fix transport-level head-of-line blocking and speed up handshakes
Check which protocol version real sites negotiate using browser devtools or curl
criteria: You can explain head-of-line blocking at both the HTTP and TCP levels
You can state why HTTP/3 uses UDP instead of TCP
You verified the negotiated HTTP version for three major websites

sections.title: Tools of the Trade
sections.description: Get hands-on with the command-line and capture tools professionals use to interrogate networks every day.

topics.title: curl Mastery
topics.description: The universal HTTP(S) client: crafting requests, setting headers and bodies, following redirects, and debugging APIs from the terminal.
topics.why: curl is the lingua franca of API debugging — bug reports, docs, and runbooks all speak it. Deep curl fluency means you can reproduce and isolate any HTTP issue in seconds without writing code.
learn: Send GET/POST/PUT requests with custom headers and JSON bodies
Use -v and -i to inspect request/response headers and TLS details
Follow redirects (-L), send cookies (-b/-c), and authenticate (-u, bearer headers)
Time and dissect a request with -w and --resolve for testing DNS/host overrides
criteria: You can reproduce any API call from browser devtools as a curl command
You debugged a redirect chain and a failing TLS connection with curl -v
You can override DNS resolution for a request without editing /etc/hosts

topics.title: dig & DNS Debugging
topics.description: Interrogating DNS from the command line: dig for precise queries, tracing delegation, checking propagation, and comparing resolvers.
topics.why: When DNS breaks, dig is how you prove where. Querying specific record types against specific servers separates resolver caching issues from authoritative misconfigurations instantly.
learn: Query specific record types: `dig example.com A`, `MX`, `TXT`, `NS`
Query a specific server (`dig @1.1.1.1`) to compare resolver answers
Trace the full delegation chain with `dig +trace`
Read the answer sections, TTLs, and flags (AA, RD, RA) in dig output
criteria: You can query any record type against any resolver from memory
You used +trace to walk a delegation and explained each step
You diagnosed a stale-cache vs wrong-record situation by comparing resolvers

topics.title: ping, traceroute, ss & netcat
topics.description: The core connectivity toolkit: ping for reachability, traceroute for path discovery, ss for socket state, and netcat for raw TCP/UDP conversations.
topics.why: These four tools answer the first questions of any incident: is the host up, where does the path break, is the port listening, can I actually connect? They work everywhere, with no installation drama.
learn: Use ping to test reachability and interpret latency and packet loss
Run traceroute and explain how TTL expiry reveals each hop
List listening ports and connection states with `ss -tulpn` and `ss -t state established`
Use netcat to test open ports, chat over raw TCP, and mock a quick server
criteria: You can explain how traceroute uses the TTL field
You verified whether a service was listening and reachable using ss and netcat
You can pick the right first tool for 'I can't reach host X' and justify it

topics.title: Wireshark & Packet Analysis
topics.description: Capturing and dissecting live traffic with Wireshark and tcpdump: filters, following streams, and reading protocol layers directly off the wire.
topics.why: Packet captures are ground truth — logs can lie, but the wire cannot. Being able to capture and filter traffic makes you the person who resolves the mystery bugs everyone else is guessing at.
learn: Capture traffic on an interface and apply capture vs display filters
Filter by host, port, and protocol (e.g. `tcp.port == 443`, `dns`)
Follow a TCP stream and identify the handshake, data, and teardown you studied
Capture on a server with tcpdump and analyze the pcap in Wireshark locally
criteria: You captured a full TCP handshake and labeled every packet
You can write display filters for a given host, port, and protocol
You moved a tcpdump capture from a remote box into Wireshark and answered a question with it

sections.title: Sockets Programming
sections.description: Turn theory into code: build real network programs on the sockets API, from echo servers to a working HTTP server.

topics.title: The Sockets API
topics.description: The classic Berkeley sockets interface: socket, bind, listen, accept, connect, send/recv — the API every language wraps.
topics.why: Every HTTP library, database driver, and RPC framework bottoms out in these calls. Knowing the raw API demystifies connection errors, lets you read systems code, and makes any language's networking layer feel familiar.
learn: Learn the lifecycle calls: socket, bind, listen, accept (server) and connect (client)
Understand address structures and what SO_REUSEADDR fixes
Grasp that TCP is a byte stream: send/recv boundaries do not equal message boundaries
Map the C API onto your favorite language's socket module
criteria: You can list the server-side socket lifecycle calls in order and explain each
You can explain why recv may return partial messages and how to handle it
You can explain what 'address already in use' means and two ways to resolve it

topics.title: Build a TCP Client & Server
topics.description: Write a working echo server and client from scratch: accept connections, exchange data, frame messages, and shut down cleanly.
topics.why: This is the 'hello world' of network programming — and the moment handshakes, ports, and byte streams stop being trivia and become muscle memory. Everything from chat apps to databases starts here.
learn: Write a TCP echo server that accepts a connection and returns what it receives
Write the matching client, then test both with netcat as counterpart
Implement a simple message-framing scheme (length prefix or delimiter)
Handle disconnects and errors gracefully; watch your traffic in Wireshark
criteria: You built a working echo server and client that talk to each other
Your server interoperates with netcat as a client
You implemented message framing and can explain why it is necessary

topics.title: UDP Sockets
topics.description: Datagram programming with sendto/recvfrom: connectionless messaging, handling loss, and building a tiny reliability layer of your own.
topics.why: UDP programming teaches you what TCP quietly does for you — and it is the required foundation for real-time apps, custom protocols, and understanding QUIC. Building mini-reliability yourself is the best TCP lesson there is.
learn: Write a UDP echo server and client using sendto/recvfrom
Observe datagram boundaries: one send equals one receive, unlike TCP
Simulate packet loss and add sequence numbers plus retry-on-timeout
Compare your code's behavior to the TCP version under an unreliable network
criteria: You built a working UDP client/server pair
You added sequence numbers and retransmission to survive simulated loss
You can articulate two behavioral differences you observed between TCP and UDP sockets

topics.title: Build an HTTP Server from Scratch
topics.description: Implement a minimal HTTP/1.1 server on raw TCP sockets: parse requests, serve files, and return correct status codes — no frameworks allowed.
topics.why: Nothing cements HTTP and sockets knowledge like implementing the protocol yourself. Afterward, every framework, reverse proxy, and load balancer behavior makes sense, because you have been on the other side of the request line.
learn: Parse the request line and headers from a raw TCP stream
Serve static files with correct Content-Type and Content-Length headers
Return proper status codes: 200, 404, 405, 500
Test your server with curl and a real browser, then add keep-alive support
criteria: A real browser can load a static page from your server
Your server returns correct status codes and headers verified with curl -v
You can explain how your parser finds the end of headers and reads the body

topics.title: Concurrency & Non-Blocking I/O
topics.description: Handling many connections at once: threads vs event loops, blocking vs non-blocking sockets, and readiness APIs like select and epoll.
topics.why: One-connection-at-a-time servers fall over immediately in the real world. The C10K ideas here are the foundation of nginx, Node.js, and every async runtime — understanding them lets you choose and tune server architectures deliberately.
learn: Make your echo or HTTP server handle multiple clients with threads
Rebuild it with an event loop using select/epoll or your language's async framework
Understand blocking vs non-blocking sockets and what 'readiness' means
Benchmark both versions under concurrent load and compare resource usage
criteria: Your server handles at least 100 simultaneous clients without dropping connections
You built both a threaded and an event-loop version and can compare their trade-offs
You can explain the difference between blocking, non-blocking, and async I/O
